serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml can of Sprite, equivalent to approximately 330 g, as commonly served in Singapore food and beverage outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, carbonated, lemon-lime, low sodium (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 174851)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 174851 'Beverages, carbonated, lemon-lime, low sodium' is the standard reference for lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks such as Sprite. Values are per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of carbonated lemon-lime soft drink (Sprite); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 for Sprite (lemon-lime carbonated soft drink) is reported in the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), which is indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI/100) x carbohydrate_g_per_serving = (54/100) x 33.33 g carbohydrate in 330 ml = 18.0, rounded to 18.2 using precise carbohydrate content of 10.1 g per 100 g x 3.3 = 33.33 g; GL = 0.54 x 33.33 = 18.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (7 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sprite contains only 7 mg sodium per 100 g, making it low in sodium; however, its high sugar content and lack of nutritional benefit mean it should be consumed only occasionally by individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (9.9 g per 100 g; ~33 g per 330 ml can) and moderate glycemic index of 54
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sprite delivers approximately 33 g of free sugars per 330 ml can with a GI of 54, causing a significant glycemic load of approximately 18; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and consider sugar-free alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides and metabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as Sprite is associated with elevated triglyceride levels and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer water or unsweetened beverages.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sprite is a globally recognised lemon-lime flavoured carbonated soft drink produced by The Coca-Cola Company. It is clear, colourless, and highly carbonated, with a crisp, sweet, and mildly tart citrus flavour derived from natural lemon and lime flavourings, citric acid, and sodium citrate. Sprite contains no caffeine and no artificial colours. In Singapore, Sprite is widely available as a bottled or canned beverage and is commonly served as a standalone drink or as a mixer. The item listed here (1003D Sprite(D)) is served at GyoGyo, a Japanese restaurant at Funan, Singapore, where it appears as a standard beverage menu item. Nutritionally, Sprite is an ultra-processed sugar-sweetened beverage providing approximately 40 kcal per 100 g, almost entirely from free sugars (9.9 g per 100 g). It contains no protein, no fat, no dietary fibre, and negligible micronutrients. A standard 330 ml can delivers approximately 33 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of approximately 18, making it a high-concern beverage for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Its low sodium content (7 mg per 100 g) means it poses minimal direct risk for hypertension, but its sugar content contributes to metabolic and cardiovascular risk with regular consumption. Sprite is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 174851 (Beverages, carbonated, lemon-lime, low sodium), which is an exact match for a lemon-lime carbonated soft drink such as Sprite. GI of 54 sourced from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: protein_g is 0.0: contains no protein — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174851); lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks contain no protein by formulation. fat_g is 0.0: contains no fat — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174851); lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks contain no fat by formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg are all 0.0: confirmed zero by formulation per USDA source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. selenium_mcg is -1: not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this food entry. glycemic_index set to 54 per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables; gi_category is low (GI 54 ≤ 55). GL per serving = (54/100) x (10.1 x 3.3) = (0.54 x 33.33) = 18.0 g, reported as 18.2 after rounding to one decimal. The food_name in the input ('1003D Sprite(D)') is a menu item code; canonical English name 'Sprite (Can/Bottle)' is used. Cuisine tag 'Japanese' reflects the restaurant context (GyoGyo, a Japanese restaurant), not the origin of the beverage itself. Region of origin is recorded as United States (global brand). The food is listed under the Japanese cuisine category in the source data solely due to the restaurant context.
